description:
You are working as a compliance analyst and must produce a tiny, completely reproducible audit-trail for the OpenSSL package that change-management can archive.  
Generate one text file, using **no more than four separate shell commands** in total.  All work must be done as the normal user.

1. Create a directory  
   /home/user/compliance

2. Inside that directory create the file  
   /home/user/compliance/openssl_audit.log

3. The log must contain **exactly three non-empty lines** in the precise order shown below; no blank lines before, after, or in-between, and lines must end with a single UNIX newline (`\n`):

   • Line 1 – the UTC time when the log is created, formatted exactly as produced by  
     `date -u +"%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ"`  
     (e.g. `2024-05-12T16:23:45Z`).  
     The literal text `TIMESTAMP=` must come first, immediately followed by the timestamp:

     TIMESTAMP=2024-05-12T16:23:45Z

   • Line 2 – the currently installed version of OpenSSL, obtained with  
     `dpkg-query -W -f='${Version}' openssl`  
     The line must start with the literal text `INSTALLED=` and then the version string exactly as returned:

     INSTALLED=1.1.1f-1ubuntu2.19

   • Line 3 – the repository’s candidate version, obtained with  
     `apt-cache policy openssl | grep -E 'Candidate:' | awk '{print $2}'`  
     The line must start with the literal text `CANDIDATE=` and then the version string exactly as returned:

     CANDIDATE=1.1.1f-1ubuntu2.19

4. Nothing else is to be written to the file—no extra spaces, comments, or trailing blank lines.

5. When you are done, you should be able to display the file with:  
   cat /home/user/compliance/openssl_audit.log  
   and see the three-line output in the exact format just described.

Hints (do NOT show actual commands here):
• You will need to refresh package metadata before querying candidate versions.  
• Use shell redirection to write the log exactly once, or collect the three values into variables before writing the file so you avoid accidental extra whitespace or lines.

The automated tests will verify:

a. /home/user/compliance exists as a directory.  
b. /home/user/compliance/openssl_audit.log exists, is a regular file, and is world-readable.  
c. The file contains exactly three lines.  
d. Line 1 begins with “TIMESTAMP=” and the remainder matches the RFC-3339 UTC format shown above.  
e. Line 2 begins with “INSTALLED=” and the version equals the output of `dpkg-query -W -f='${Version}' openssl` executed in the test environment.  
f. Line 3 begins with “CANDIDATE=” and the version equals the “Candidate:” value from `apt-cache policy openssl` executed in the test environment.  
g. There are no extra characters (spaces, tabs, carriage returns) before or after any of the three lines.

Remember: you must accomplish all of the above using at most four shell commands.
truth:
Directory that must exist after completion:
  /home/user/compliance        (permissions 755, owned by user)

File that must exist after completion:
  /home/user/compliance/openssl_audit.log  (permissions 644, owned by user)

The file must contain exactly three lines terminated by '\n'.  
Patterns the tester will match (POSIX extended regex, whole line):

1. ^TIMESTAMP=[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}T[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}:[0-9]{2}Z$
2. ^INSTALLED=$(dpkg-query -W -f='${Version}' openssl)$
3. ^CANDIDATE=$(apt-cache policy openssl | awk '/Candidate:/ {print $2}')$

No additional lines or characters allowed.
